Update the winner to in_progress with assignee=worker\n53\t/// 4. Insert an event\n54\t/// 5. Mark the bead as dirty\n55\t/// 6. claimed\n65\t/// * `Ok(None)` - No beads available to claim\n66\t/// * `Err(e)` - Transaction error\n67\tpub fn claim(\n68\t    tx: &Connection,\n69\t    worker: &str,\n70\t    claim_ttl_minutes: anyhow::Result;\nuse chrono::{DateTime, Duration, Utc};\nuse rusqlite::{params, Connection};\nuse serde::Serialize;\n\n/// Result of a claim operation\n#[derive(Debug, Clone)]\npub struct ClaimResult {\n    pub bead_id: String,\n    pub reclaimed: usize,\n}\n\n/// Score for cross-workspace candidate comparison.\n///\n/// Higher scores are better. Ordered by:\n/// 1. downstream_impact (more blocking = higher priority)\n/// 2. negative critical_float (lower float = more critical)\n/// 3. negative priority (lower number = higher priority)\n/// 4. negative created timestamp (older = higher priority/FIFO)\n#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, PartialOrd, Ord)]\npub struct Score {\n    pub downstream_impact: i64,\n    pub critical_float: i64,\n    pub priority: i32,\n    pub created_at_ts: i64,\n}\n\nimpl Score {\n    /// Create a new score from candidate fields.\n    pub fn new(downstream_impact: i64, critical_float: i64, priority: i32, created_at_ts: i64) -> Self {\n        Self { downstream_impact, critical_float, priority, created_at_ts }\n    }\n}\n\n/// A bead with its score for ready/claim operations\n#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize)]\npub struct ScoredBead {\n    pub id: String,\n    pub title: String,\n    pub status: String,\n    pub priority: i32,\n    pub downstream_impact: i64,\n    pub critical_float: f64,\n    pub created_at: String,\n}\n\n/// Atomically claim a bead for a worker.\n///\n/// This function performs the following in a single IMMEDIATE transaction:\n/// 1. Reclaim stale in_progress beads (older than claim_ttl_minutes) back to open\n/// 2. Select candidates with downstream_impact + critical_float scoring\n/// 3. Update the winner to in_progress with assignee=worker\n/// 4. Insert an event\n/// 5. Mark the bead as dirty\n/// 6. Commit\n///\n/// # Arguments\n/// * `tx` - The transaction to use (must be an IMMEDIATE transaction)\n/// * `worker` - The worker ID claiming the bead\n/// * `claim_ttl_minutes` - TTL in minutes after which in_progress beads are reclaimed\n/// * `now` - Current timestamp for staleness calculation\n///\n/// # Returns\n/// * `Ok(Some(claim_result))` - A bead was claimed\n/// * `Ok(None)` - No beads available to claim\n/// * `Err(e)` - Transaction error\npub fn claim(\n    tx: &Connection,\n    worker: &str,\n    claim_ttl_minutes: i64,\n    now: DateTime<Utc>,\n) -> Result<Option<ClaimResult>> {\n    // Step 1: Reclaim stale in_progress beads\n    let stale_cutoff = now - Duration::minutes(claim_ttl_minutes);\n    let reclaimed = tx.execute(\n        \"UPDATE issues\n         SET status = 'open', assignee = NULL, updated_at = ?\n         WHERE status = 'in_progress'\n           AND updated_at < ?\",\n        params![now.to_rfc3339(), stale_cutoff.to_rfc3339()],\n    )?;\n\n    // Step 2: Find candidate beads with impact scoring\n    // Score = downstream_impact + (critical_float / 1000.0)\n    // downstream_impact = count of beads blocked by this one\n    // critical_float = from critical_path_cache (lower is more critical)\n    let mut stmt = tx.prepare(\n        \"SELECT i.id,\n                COALESCE(COUNT(d.issue_id), 0) as downstream_impact,\n                COALESCE(c.float, 999999) as critical_float,\n                i.priority\n         FROM issues i\n         LEFT JOIN dependencies d ON d.depends_on_id = i.id AND d.type IN ('blocks', 'parent-child', 'conditional-blocks', 'waits-for')\n         LEFT JOIN critical_path_cache c ON c.bead_id = i.id\n         WHERE i.status = 'open'\n           AND i.ephemeral = 0\n           AND i.pinned = 0\n           AND i.is_template = 0\n           AND i.deleted_at IS NULL\n         GROUP BY i.id\n         ORDER BY\n             downstream_impact DESC,\n             critical_float ASC,\n             i.priority ASC,\n             i.created_at ASC\n         LIMIT 1\",\n    )?;\n\n    let mut rows = stmt.query([])?;\n\n    if let Some(row) = rows.next()? {\n        let bead_id: String = row.get(0)?;\n\n        // Step 3: Update the winner to in_progress with a race condition check\n        // The WHERE status = 'open' condition ensures we only claim if still open\n        let rows_affected = tx.execute(\n            \"UPDATE issues\n             SET status = 'in_progress', assignee = ?, updated_at = ?\n             WHERE id = ? AND status = 'open'\",\n            params![worker, now.to_rfc3339(), &bead_id],\n        )?;\n\n        // If no rows were affected, another worker claimed this bead first\n        if rows_affected == 0 {\n            return Ok(None);\n        }\n\n        // Step 4: Insert event\n        tx.execute(\n            \"INSERT INTO events (issue_id, event_type, actor, new_value, created_at)\n             VALUES (?, 'assignee_changed', '', ?, ?)\",\n            params![&bead_id, worker, now.to_rfc3339()],\n        )?;\n\n        // Step 5: Mark as dirty\n        tx.execute(\n            \"INSERT OR REPLACE INTO dirty_issues (issue_id, marked_at)\n             VALUES (?, ?)\",\n            params![&bead_id, now.to_rfc3339()],\n        )?;\n\n        Ok(Some(ClaimResult {\n            bead_id,\n            reclaimed,\n        }))\n    } else {\n        Ok(None)\n    }\n}\n\n/// Get ready candidates using the same scoring logic as claim().\n///\n/// This returns a list of beads that would be considered for claiming,\n/// ordered by the same scoring formula:\n/// - downstream_impact DESC (more blocking = higher priority)\n/// - critical_float ASC (lower float = more critical)\n/// - priority ASC (0=Critical, 4=Backlog)\n/// - created_at ASC (FIFO tiebreaker)\n///\n/// # Arguments\n/// * `tx` - The transaction to use\n/// * `limit` - Maximum number of candidates to return\n///\n/// # Returns\n/// * `Ok(Vec<ScoredBead>)` - List of scored bead candidates\npub fn get_ready_candidates(tx: &Connection, limit: usize) -> Result<Vec<ScoredBead>> {\n    let mut stmt = tx.prepare(\n        \"SELECT i.id, i.title, i.status, i.priority,\n                COALESCE(COUNT(d.issue_id), 0) as downstream_impact,\n                COALESCE(c.float, 999999) as critical_float,\n                i.created_at\n         FROM issues i\n         LEFT JOIN dependencies d ON d.depends_on_id = i.id AND d.type IN ('blocks', 'parent-child', 'conditional-blocks', 'waits-for')\n         LEFT JOIN critical_path_cache c ON c.bead_id = i.id\n         WHERE i.status = 'open'\n           AND i.ephemeral = 0\n           AND i.pinned = 0\n           AND i.is_template = 0\n           AND i.deleted_at IS NULL\n         GROUP BY i.id\n         ORDER BY\n             downstream_impact DESC,\n             critical_float ASC,\n             i.priority ASC,\n             i.created_at ASC\n         LIMIT ?1\",\n    )?;\n\n    let mut rows = stmt.query(params![limit as i64])?;\n    let mut candidates = Vec::new();\n\n    while let Some(row) = rows.next()? {\n        candidates.push(ScoredBead {\n            id: row.get(0)?,\n            title: row.get(1)?,\n            status: row.get(2)?,\n            priority: row.get(3)?,\n            downstream_impact: row.get(4)?,\n            critical_float: row.get(5)?,\n            created_at: row.get(6)?,\n        });\n    }\n\n    Ok(candidates)\n}\n\n#[cfg(test)]\nmod tests {\n    use super::*;\n    use crate::storage::Storage;\n    use crate::model::{Issue, Status};\n\n    fn setup_test_db() -> (tempfile::NamedTempFile, Storage) {\n        let temp_file = tempfile::NamedTempFile::new().unwrap();\n        let storage = Storage::open(temp_file.path()).unwrap();\n        (temp_file, storage)\n    }\n\n    #[test]\n    fn test_claim_basic() {\n        let (_temp, mut storage) = setup_test_db();\n\n        // Create an open bead\n        let issue = Issue::new(\"bf-test1\".to_string(), \"Test bead\".to_string(), \".\".to_string());\n        storage.create_issue(&issue).unwrap();\n\n        // Claim it\n        let result = storage.with_immediate_transaction(|tx| {\n            claim(tx, \"worker1\", 30, Utc::now())\n        }).unwrap();\n\n        assert!(result.is_some());\n        let claim_result = result.unwrap();\n        assert_eq!(claim_result.bead_id, \"bf-test1\");\n        assert_eq!(claim_result.reclaimed, 0);\n\n        // Verify the bead is now in_progress\n        let updated = storage.get_issue(\"bf-test1\").unwrap().unwrap();\n        assert_eq!(updated.status, Status::InProgress);\n        assert_eq!(updated.assignee.as_ref().unwrap(), \"worker1\");\n    }\n\n    #[test]\n    fn test_claim_no_candidates() {\n        let (_temp, mut storage) = setup_test_db();\n\n        // No beads available\n        let result = storage.with_immediate_transaction(|tx| {\n            claim(tx, \"worker1\", 30, Utc::now())\n        }).unwrap();\n\n        assert!(result.is_none());\n    }\n\n    #[test]\n    fn test_claim_reclaims_stale() {\n        let (_temp, mut storage) = setup_test_db();\n\n        // Create an in_progress bead with old updated_at\n        let mut issue = Issue::new(\"bf-stale\".to_string(), \"Stale bead\".to_string(), \".\".to_string());\n storage.create_issue(&issue).unwrap();\n\n        // Create an open bead\n        let issue2 = Issue::new(\"bf-open\".to_string(), \"Open bead\".to_string(), \".\".to_string());\n        storage.create_issue(&issue2).unwrap();\n\n        // Claim with 30 min TTL - should reclaim the stale one\n        let result = storage.with_immediate_transaction(|tx| {\n            claim(tx, \"worker_new\", 30, Utc::now())\n        }).unwrap();\n\n        assert!(result.is_some());\n        let claim_result = result.unwrap();\n        assert_eq!(claim_result.reclaimed, 1);\n\n        // The open bead should be claimed (not the stale one, since it was reclaimed to open)\n        // After reclaim, the stale bead is open again, so it could be claimed too\n        // But the open bead has priority by created_at order\n    }\n}\n","numLines":284,"startLine":1,"totalLines":284}}}
{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"call_7671978a99e7461789e2b587","type":"tool_result","content":"1\t# bead-forge Implementation Plan\n2\t\n3\t> **User-facing documentation:** [`../README.md`](../README.md) — overview, command reference, NEEDLE integration guide, and hero image.\n4\t\n5\t## Overview\n6\t\n7\t**bead-forge** (`bf`) is a Rust binary that replaces `br` (beads_rust). SQLite is the live database — all reads and writes go through it. `issues.jsonl` is a git-synced artifact exported on demand: one entry per bead, committed to the repo for backup, sharing, and recovery. Concurrent claiming is handled by SQLite's `BEGIN IMMEDIATE` transaction — no server, no daemon, no flock required.\n8\t\n9\t## Core Principles\n10\t\n11\t1. **SQLite is the live store** — all mutations are SQL writes; reads are SQL queries. Fast, concurrent-read, serialized-write via WAL mode.\n12\t2. **JSONL is the git artifact** — exported on `bf sync`, committed to git, one entry per bead. Used for backup, cross-machine sharing, and recovery. Never written during normal operation.\n13\t3. **Drop-in CLI** — all `br` commands work under `bf` with the same flags and output formats\n14\t4. **`BEGIN IMMEDIATE` for atomic claim** — the read-score-update sequence runs inside a single SQLite write transaction, eliminating the race condition `br` has without requiring flock or a daemon\n15\t\n16\t---\n17\t\n18\t## Phase 1: Core Library — Data Model & JSONL Import/Export\n19\t\n20\t### 1.1 Data Model (`src/model.rs`)\n21\t\n22\tPort the exact `Issue`, `Status`, `Priority`, `IssueType`, `Dependency`, `DependencyType`, `Comment`, and `Event` structs from beads_rust with identical Serde attributes. This is the wire format — every field, every `skip_serializing_if`, every rename must match.\n23\t\n24\tKey structs and their JSONL serialization:\n25\t\n26\t```\n27\tStatus:       open | in_progress | blocked | deferred | draft | closed | tombstone | pinned | Custom(String)\n28\tPriority:     transparent i32 (0=Critical, 4=Backlog)\n29\tIssueType:    task | bug | feature | epic | chore | docs | question | Custom(String)\n30\tDependency:   { issue_id, depends_on_id, type (renamed from dep_type), created_at, created_by?, metadata?, thread_id? }\n31\tComment:      { id: i64, issue_id, author, text (renamed from body), created_at }\n32\tEvent:        { id: i64, issue_id, event_type, actor, old_value?, new_value?, comment?, created_at }\n33\t```\n34\t\n35\tIssue fields (alphabetical by JSON key):\n36\t```\n37\tid, title, description?, design?, acceptance_criteria?, notes?,\n38\tstatus, priority, issue_type, assignee?, owner?, estimated_minutes?,\n39\tcreated_at, created_by?, updated_at, closed_at?, close_reason?,\n40\tclosed_by_session?, due_at?, defer_until?, external_ref?,\n41\tsource_system?, source_repo, deleted_at?, deleted_by?, delete_reason?,\n42\toriginal_type?, compaction_level (default 0), compacted_at?,\n43\tcompacted_at_commit?, original_size?, sender?, ephemeral (default false),\n44\tpinned (default false), is_template (default false),\n45\tlabels: Vec<String>, dependencies: Vec<Dependency>, comments: Vec<Comment>\n46\t```\n47\t\n48\t### 1.2 JSONL Import (`src/jsonl.rs`)\n49\t\n50\tJSONL is read only during `bf sync --import` (pulling state from git) and `bf doctor --repair` (rebuilding SQLite from the artifact). Never read during normal CLI operation.\n51\t\n52\t```rust\n53\tfn import_jsonl(jsonl_path: &Path, storage: &Storage) -> Result<ImportResult> {\n54\t    let file = File::open(jsonl_path)?;\n55\t    let reader = BufReader::new(file);\n56\t    storage.with_write_transaction(|tx| {\n57\t        for line in reader.lines() {\n58\t            let issue: Issue = serde_json::from_str(&line?)?;\n59\t            tx.upsert_issue(&issue)?;\n60\t        }\n61\t        Ok(())\n62\t    })\n63\t}\n64\t```\n65\t\n66\tFunctions:\n67\t- `import_jsonl(path, storage)` — bulk upsert from JSONL into SQLite\n68\t- `stream_issues(path) -> impl Iterator<Item = Result<Issue>>` — lazy line iterator (used by import and doctor)\n69\t\n70\t### 1.3 JSONL Export (`src/jsonl.rs`)\n71\t\n72\tJSONL is written only during `bf sync --flush` (preparing the git artifact). Exports current bead state from SQLite, one entry per bead, sorted by ID for stable diffs. Atomic temp+rename.\n73\t\n74\t```rust\n75\tfn export_jsonl(jsonl_path: &Path, storage: &Storage) -> Result<ExportResult> {\n76\t    let issues = storage.list_all_issues()?;  // sorted by ID\n77\t    let temp_path = jsonl_path.with_extension(\"jsonl.tmp\");\n78\t    let mut writer = BufWriter::new(File::create_new(&temp_path)?);\n79\t    for issue in &issues {\n80\t        serde_json::to_writer(&mut writer, issue)?;\n81\t        writer.write_all(b\"\\n\")?;\n82\t    }\n83\t    writer.flush()?;\n84\t    drop(writer);\n85\t    fs::rename(&temp_path, jsonl_path)?;\n86\t    Ok(ExportResult { count: issues.len() })\n87\t}\n88\t```\n89\t\n90\tFunctions:\n91\t- `export_jsonl(path, storage)` — dump all beads from SQLite to JSONL (atomic)\n92\t- `export_jsonl_dirty(path, storage)` — export only dirty beads (incremental, faster for large workspaces)\n93\t\n94\t### 1.4 Config (`src/config.rs`)\n95\t\n96\tParse `.beads/config.yaml` with the same fields br uses:\n97\t\n98\t```yaml\n99\tissue_prefixes: [bd, fg]\n100\tdefault_priority: 2\n101\tdefault_type: task\n102\tscoring:\n103\t  priority_weight: 0.4\n104\t  blockers_weight: 0.3\n105\t  age_weight: 0.2\n106\t  labels_weight: 0.1\n107\t  max_age_hours: 20\n108\t  max_blockers: 3\n109\t# bead-forge additions (additive, br ignores these)\n110\tclaim_ttl_minutes: 30\n111\t```\n112\t\n113\tParse `.beads/metadata.json`:\n114\t```json\n115\t{ \"database\": \"beads.db\", \"jsonl_export\": \"issues.jsonl\" }\n116\t```\n117\t\n118\t### 1.5 ID Generation (`src/id.rs`)\n119\t\n120\tPort br's ID scheme: `<prefix>-<hash>` where hash is base36 lowercase. Use the birthday-problem adaptive length (3-8 chars). Read `issue_prefixes` from config (first entry is the default prefix).\n121\t\n122\t```rust\n123\tfn generate_id(prefix: &str, existing_count: usize) -> String {\n124\t    let len = optimal_hash_length(existing_count);\n125\t    let random_bytes = rand::random::<[u8; 16]>();\n126\t    let hash = sha256(&random_bytes);\n127\t    base36_encode(&hash[..len])\n128\t    format!(\"{}-{}\", prefix, hash)\n129\t}\n130\t```\n131\t\n132\t---\n133\t\n134\t## Phase 2: SQLite Primary Store\n135\t\n136\t### 2.1 Schema Application (`src/storage/schema.rs`)\n137\t\n138\tApply the exact same `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS` DDL from br's `SCHEMA_SQL`. Tables:\n139\t\n140\t| Table | Purpose |\n141\t|-------|---------|\n142\t| `issues` | All 35 columns, same types, defaults, CHECK constraints |\n143\t| `dependencies` | (issue_id, depends_on_id) PK, type, metadata, thread_id |\n144\t| `labels` | (issue_id, label) PK |\n145\t| `comments` | AUTOINCREMENT id, issue_id, author, text, created_at |\n146\t| `events` | AUTOINCREMENT id, issue_id, event_type, actor, old/new/comment |\n147\t| `config` | key-value |\n148\t| `metadata` | key-value |\n149\t| `dirty_issues` | Track which issues need JSONL export |\n150\t| `export_hashes` | Incremental export tracking |\n151\t| `blocked_issues_cache` | Materialized view of blocked issues |\n152\t| `child_counters` | Hierarchical IDs (bd-abc.1, bd-abc.2) |\n153\t| `recovery_sessions` | Anomaly/recovery audit trail |\n154\t| `anomaly_audit` | Recovery event tracking |\n155\t| `bead_annotations` | bf-only: arbitrary key-value metadata per bead; separate table so br's `rebuild_issues_table()` never touches it |\n156\t\n157\tAll indexes from br must be created identically, including the critical `idx_issues_ready` composite partial index.\n158\t\n159\t### 2.2 Storage Engine (`src/storage/sqlite.rs`)\n160\t\n161\tUse `rusqlite` (not FrankenSQLite) bundled with system SQLite. Apply WAL mode, foreign keys, busy_timeout=30s, cache_size=-8000, synchronous=NORMAL.\n162\t\n163\tAll mutations go through here. JSONL is never touched during normal operation.\n164\t\n165\tFunctions:\n166\t- `open(path: &Path) -> Result<Storage>` — open/create DB, apply schema\n167\t- `get_issue(id: &str) -> Result<Option<Issue>>` — point lookup by ID\n168\t- `list_issues(filter: IssueFilter) -> Result<Vec<Issue>>` — filtered query with WHERE clauses\n169\t- `list_all_issues() -> Result<Vec<Issue>>` — full table scan sorted by ID (for JSONL export)\n170\t- `create_issue(issue: &Issue) -> Result<()>` — INSERT\n171\t- `update_issue(id: &str, changes: IssueChanges) -> Result<()>` — UPDATE + mark dirty\n172\t- `close_issue(id: &str, reason: &str) -> Result<()>` — status transition + mark dirty\n173\t- `with_write_transaction(f) -> Result<T>` — execute closure in a write transaction\n174\t- `with_immediate_transaction(f) -> Result<T>` — `BEGIN IMMEDIATE` with retry (see §2.5) — acquires write lock before any reads (used by claim)\n175\t- `rebuild_blocked_cache() -> Result<()>` — recalculate blocked issues materialized view\n176\t\n177\t### 2.3 Sync Protocol (`src/sync.rs`)\n178\t\n179\tPort br's sync semantics:\n180\t\n181\t**Flush (SQLite → JSONL, for git)**:\n182\t1. Call `export_jsonl_dirty` — query dirty bead IDs, export full current state of each from SQLite\n183\t2. Atomic temp+rename over `issues.jsonl`\n184\t3. Clear `dirty_issues`, update `export_hashes`\n185\t\n186\tFor `bf sync` (full flush): call `export_jsonl` — dump all beads sorted by ID, unconditionally.\n187\t\n188\t**Import (JSONL → SQLite, from git pull)**:\n189\t1. Stream `issues.jsonl` line by line\n190\t2. Compare each with SQLite state using `content_hash`\n191\t3. INSERT new, UPDATE changed, SKIP unchanged\n192\t4. Handle collisions (same ID, different content) with deterministic resolution\n193\t\n194\t### 2.5 Concurrent Write Safety (`src/storage/sqlite.rs`)\n195\t\n196\tSQLite WAL mode serializes concurrent writers through a single write lock — they queue, not corrupt. `SQLITE_BUSY` is returned when a writer cannot acquire the lock within `busy_timeout`. With claim latency of ~1-2ms and a 20-worker fleet, the entire queue clears in ~20-40ms, so `SQLITE_BUSY` should never occur in normal operation. However, a retry wrapper is the cheap safety net that makes this a guarantee rather than an assumption:\n197\t\n198\t```rust\n199\tconst MAX_RETRIES: u32 = 5;\n200\tconst RETRY_BASE_MS: u64 = 50;\n201\t\n202\tfn with_immediate_transaction<T>(\n203\t    conn: &Connection,\n204\t    f: impl Fn(&Transaction) -> Result<T>,\n205\t) -> Result<T> {\n206\t    let mut attempt = 0;\n207\t    loop {\n208\t        match conn.execute(\"BEGIN IMMEDIATE\", []) {\n209\t            Ok(_) => {\n210\t                let tx = conn.unchecked_transaction()?;\n211\t                return match f(&tx) {\n212\t                    Ok(val) => { tx.commit()?; Ok(val) }\n213\t                    Err(e) => { let _ = tx.rollback(); Err(e) }\n214\t                };\n215\t            }\n216\t            Err(e) if is_busy_error(&e) && attempt < MAX_RETRIES => {\n217\t                attempt += 1;\n218\t                std::thread::sleep(Duration::from_millis(RETRY_BASE_MS * attempt as u64));\n219\t                continue;\n220\t            }\n221\t            Err(e) => return Err(e.into()),\n222\t        }\n223\t    }\n224\t}\n225\t\n226\tfn is_busy_error(e: &rusqlite::Error) -> bool {\n227\t    matches!(\n228\t        e,\n229\t        rusqlite::Error::SqliteFailure(\n230\t            rusqlite::ffi::Error { code: rusqlite::ErrorCode::DatabaseBusy, .. },\n231\t            _\n232\t        )\n233\t    )\n234\t}\n235\t```\n236\t\n237\t**Why this is sufficient and not a workaround**: `SQLITE_BUSY` with WAL mode is not data corruption — it is a clean \"lock unavailable, try again\" signal. The database is fully consistent when this occurs. The retry converts a transient contention spike (e.g., 20 workers all starting within the same millisecond) into a short wait rather than a user-visible error.\n238\t\n239\t**All write operations** (`claim`, `create`, `update`, `close`, `batch`) use `with_immediate_transaction`. Read-only operations (`list`, `show`, `ready`) use a plain `BEGIN DEFERRED` transaction and are never affected by write contention.\n240\t\n241\t### 2.7 Doctor/Repair (`src/doctor.rs`)\n242\t\n243\t- `doctor --repair`: Delete `beads.db`, reimport from JSONL\n244\t- `doctor --check`: Validate JSONL line integrity, check SQLite <-> JSONL consistency\n245\t- Recovery from corruption: JSONL export is always authoritative — `beads.db` can be rebuilt from it at any time\n246\t\n247\t---\n248\t\n249\t## Phase 3: CLI Commands\n250\t\n251\t### 3.1 Command Structure (`src/cli/`)\n252\t\n253\tUse `clap` with derive macros. Binary name: `bf`. Subcommands mirror br exactly.\n254\t\n255\t```\n256\tbf create    --title \"...\" --type <type> --priority <N> [--description \"...\"] [--label ...]\n257\tbf list      [--status <s>] [--type <t>] [--assignee <a>] [--limit N] [--format json|text|toon]\n258\tbf show      <id>\n259\tbf update    <id> [--status <s>] [--assignee <a>] [--priority <N>] [--title \"...\"] ...\n260\tbf close     <id> [--reason \"...\"]\n261\tbf reopen    <id>\n262\tbf delete    <id>\n263\tbf ready         [--limit N]                     # unblocked, open, sorted by priority\n264\tbf claim         --assignee <id>                 # atomic claim (see Phase 4)\n265\t                 [--model <model>]               # e.g. claude-sonnet-4-6\n266\t                 [--harness <name>]              # e.g. needle\n267\t                 [--harness-version <ver>]       # e.g. 0.5.2\n268\t                 [--workspace <path>] [--any]\n269\tbf critical-path <epic-id>                       # show critical path + float for each bead\n270\tbf dep           add-blocker <parent> <child>\n271\tbf dep           remove-blocker <parent> <child>\n272\tbf dep           tree <id>\n273\tbf label         <id> add <label>\n274\tbf label         <id> remove <label>\n275\tbf comment       <id> [--author <a>] [--text \"...\"]\n276\tbf sync          --flush-only\n277\tbf sync          --import-only\n278\tbf doctor        [--repair]\n279\tbf migrate       [<workspace>]                  # migrate br workspace to bf (see Phase 4C)\n280\t                 [--from-jsonl]                 # rebuild SQLite from JSONL + git history\n281\t                 [--seed-velocity]              # populate velocity_stats from events history\n282\t                 [--dry-run]\n283\tbf init          [--prefix <p>]\n284\tbf stats\n285\tbf count         [--status <s>]\n286\tbf search        <query>\n287\tbf schema\n288\tbf config        get <key>\n289\tbf config        set <key> <value>\n290\t```\n291\t\n292\t### 3.2 Output Formats\n293\t\n294\tThree formats, matching br exactly:\n295\t- **text**: Human-readable table (default for terminal)\n296\t- **json**: One JSON object per line (for piping)\n297\t- **toon**: Token-optimized output for LLM context windows\n298\t\n299\t### 3.3 Execution Model\n300\t\n301\tEach command:\n302\t1. Locate `.beads/` directory (walk up from CWD)\n303\t2. Load config + metadata\n304\t3. Open SQLite (`beads.db`), apply schema if new\n305\t4. Execute: mutations are SQL writes; reads are SQL queries\n306\t5. `bf sync` is the only command that touches `issues.jsonl` — flush exports it, import reads it\n307\t\n308\t---\n309\t\n310\t## Phase 4: Concurrent Claiming\n311\t\n312\t### 4.1 Architecture: SQLite `BEGIN IMMEDIATE`\n313\t\n314\tThe thundering herd problem — N workers racing to claim the same bead — is solved by running the entire read-score-update sequence inside a single `BEGIN IMMEDIATE` transaction. SQLite's write lock is acquired before any reads, so no two workers can read the same candidate state. No flock, no server, no daemon.\n315\t\n316\t```\n317\tWorker 1: BEGIN IMMEDIATE → SELECT candidates → score → UPDATE winner → COMMIT\n318\tWorker 2: BEGIN IMMEDIATE → (blocked at write lock until Worker 1 COMMITs) → SELECT → score → UPDATE → COMMIT\n319\tWorker 3: BEGIN IMMEDIATE → (blocked) → ...\n320\t...\n321\tWorker 20: BEGIN IMMEDIATE → (blocked) → SELECT → NONE (queue empty) → COMMIT (no-op)\n322\t```\n323\t\n324\tEach claim is a single CLI invocation. The critical section (lock held) is: SELECT scoring fields → score in memory → UPDATE winner + stale resets → COMMIT. Lock held for ~0.5-2ms.\n325\t\n326\t**Throughput**: SQLite handles claims purely in memory after the initial page cache warm-up:\n327\t\n328\t| Workspace | Active beads | Claim latency | Throughput |\n329\t|-----------|-------------|---------------|-----------|\n330\t| Typical (FORGE) | 248 | ~0.5ms | ~2000/sec |\n331\t| Medium (NEEDLE) | 518 | ~1ms | ~1000/sec |\n332\t| Large | 2000 | ~2ms | ~500/sec |\n333\t\n334\tWith 20 workers claiming simultaneously, the last worker waits ~20-40ms (19 queued transactions × ~1-2ms each). The fleet needs ~1-2 claims/sec at steady state — SQLite is overkill by 3 orders of magnitude.\n335\t\n336\t### 4.2 Atomic Claim (`src/claim.rs`)\n337\t\n338\tThe entire claim sequence runs inside a single `BEGIN IMMEDIATE` transaction. SQLite acquires the write lock before any reads, so no two workers can observe the same candidate state.\n339\t\n340\t```rust\n341\tfn claim(storage: &Storage, worker: &str, claim_ttl: Duration) -> Result<Option<Issue>> {\n342\t    storage.with_immediate_transaction(|tx| {\n343\t        let now = Utc::now();\n344\t\n345\t        // 1. Reclaim stale in_progress beads\n346\t        tx.execute(\n347\t            \"UPDATE issues SET status='open', assignee=NULL, updated_at=?1\n348\t             WHERE status='in_progress' AND updated_at < ?2\",\n349\t            params![now, now - claim_ttl],\n350\t        )?;\n351\t\n352\t        // 2. Load scoring fields for open, unblocked candidates\n353\t        //    (blocked_issues_cache is a materialized view maintained on dep changes)\n354\t        let candidates: Vec<ScoreEntry> = tx.query_map(\n355\t            \"SELECT id, priority, updated_at,\n356\t                    (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM dependencies d\n357\t                     JOIN issues b ON b.id = d.depends_on_id\n358\t                     WHERE d.issue_id IN (\n359\t                         SELECT issue_id FROM dependencies\n360\t                         WHERE depends_on_id = issues.id AND type='blocks'\n361\t                     ) AND b.status = 'open') AS downstream_impact\n362\t             FROM issues\n363\t             WHERE status = 'open'\n364\t               AND id NOT IN (SELECT issue_id FROM blocked_issues_cache)\n365\t             ORDER BY downstream_impact DESC, priority ASC, created_at ASC\",\n366\t            [],\n367\t            |row| ScoreEntry::from_row(row),\n368\t        )?;\n369\t\n370\t        if candidates.is_empty() {\n371\t            return Ok(None);\n372\t        }\n373\t\n374\t        // 3. Claim the top candidate\n375\t        let winner = &candidates[0];\n376\t        tx.execute(\n377\t            \"UPDATE issues SET status='in_progress', assignee=?1, updated_at=?2\n378\t             WHERE id=?3\",\n379\t            params![worker, now, winner.id],\n380\t        )?;\n381\t        tx.execute(\n382\t            \"INSERT INTO events (issue_id, event_type, actor, old_value, new_value, created_at)\n383\t             VALUES (?1, 'claimed', ?2, 'open', 'in_progress', ?3)\",\n384\t            params![winner.id, worker, now],\n385\t        )?;\n386\t        tx.mark_dirty(&winner.id)?;\n387\t\n388\t        Ok(Some(tx.get_issue(&winner.id)?))\n389\t    })\n390\t}\n391\t```\n392\t\n393\t**Key properties**:\n394\t- **Correctness**: `BEGIN IMMEDIATE` holds the SQLite write lock for the entire read-score-update sequence. No two workers can observe the same candidate state.\n395\t- **Crash safety**: If the process crashes mid-transaction, SQLite rolls back automatically. No partial state is committed.\n396\t- **Stale reclamation is a side effect**: The first statement in every claim resets expired `in_progress` beads before scoring. No separate process.\n397\t- **Impact-weighted scoring**: The query orders by `downstream_impact` (beads that would unblock the most work) then priority, then FIFO.\n398\t- **No flock, no file I/O**: The entire claim is in-memory SQL execution after page cache warm-up. `issues.jsonl` is not touched.\n399\t\n400\t### 4.3 Multi-Workspace Claiming (`src/claim.rs`)\n401\t\n402\tFor `bf claim --any` (claim from any workspace), open each workspace's SQLite, run the scoring query on each, pick the global winner, claim it:\n403\t\n404\t```rust\n405\tfn claim_any(workspace_paths: &[PathBuf], worker: &str, claim_ttl: Duration) -> Result<Option<Issue>> {\n406\t    // Score across all workspaces — each Storage::open is cheap (WAL, page cache)\n407\t    let mut best: Option<(Score, PathBuf)> = None;\n408\t    for path in workspace_paths {\n409\t        let storage = Storage::open(&path.join(\".beads/beads.db\"))?;\n410\t        if let Some(score) = storage.top_candidate_score(claim_ttl)? {\n411\t            if best.as_ref().map(|(b, _)| score > *b).unwrap_or(true) {\n412\t                best = Some((score, path.clone()));\n413\t            }\n414\t        }\n415\t    }\n416\t\n417\t    match best {\n418\t        None => Ok(None),\n419\t        Some((_, path)) => {\n420\t            let storage = Storage::open(&path.join(\".beads/beads.db\"))?;\n421\t            claim(&storage, worker, claim_ttl)\n422\t        }\n423\t    }\n424\t}\n425\t```\n426\t\n427\tNo cross-workspace locking needed — each `claim()` call uses `BEGIN IMMEDIATE` on its own SQLite file. Two workers racing on the same workspace are serialized by SQLite; two workers racing on different workspaces proceed in parallel.\n428\t\n429\t### 4.4 Stale Claim Detection\n430\t\n431\tNo background process needed. Stale detection runs as part of every `bf claim` invocation:\n432\t\n433\t1. Inside `BEGIN IMMEDIATE`: `UPDATE issues SET status='open' WHERE status='in_progress' AND updated_at < now - claim_ttl`\n434\t2. This runs before candidate scoring — stale beads become eligible again in the same transaction\n435\t3. Single SQL statement, no extra I/O\n436\t\n437\t**Claim TTL**: Default 30 minutes, configurable in `.beads/config.yaml`:\n438\t\n439\t```yaml\n440\tclaim_ttl_minutes: 30\n441\t```\n442\t\n443\t**TTL tuning**: With 20 workers and tasks taking 5-15 minutes, 30 minutes is conservative. A worker that's actively working but slow will have its bead remain `in_progress` — stale reclamation only triggers if the worker hasn't updated the bead in 30 minutes. Since the worker closes the bead when done (writing a new entry), active workers never get reclaimed.\n444\t\n445\t**Manual stale scan**: For workspaces with no active claiming (no fleet running), `bf doctor --reclaim-stale` performs the same scan without claiming a bead. Can be run via cron for long-running workspaces with occasional stuck claims.\n446\t\n447\t### 4.5 CLI Usage\n448\t\n449\t```bash\n450\t# Claim from current workspace\n451\tbf claim --assignee worker-7 --json\n452\t\n453\t# Claim with worker metadata (feeds velocity-aware scoring — see §4B.6)\n454\tbf claim --assignee worker-7 \\\n455\t         --model claude-sonnet-4-6 \\\n456\t         --harness needle \\\n457\t         --harness-version 0.5.2 \\\n458\t         --json\n459\t\n460\t# Claim from a specific workspace\n461\tbf claim --workspace containers/zai-proxy/ --assignee worker-3 --json\n462\t\n463\t# Claim highest priority across all workspaces\n464\tbf claim --any --assignee worker-12 --json\n465\t\n466\t# Prefer a workspace, fall back to any\n467\tbf claim --workspace containers/zai-proxy/ --fallback any --assignee worker-5 --json\n468\t```\n469\t\n470\tAll commands work identically in all modes — no server required, no daemon running. Each `bf claim` is a self-contained CLI invocation: lock, read, claim, write, unlock, exit.\n471\t\n472\tWorker metadata (`--model`, `--harness`, `--harness-version`) is stored in the claim event row and the `worker_sessions` table. It drives velocity-aware scoring (§4B.6) and appears in `bf log` output so you can see exactly which model/harness combination claimed each bead.\n473\t\n474\t### 4.6 Why No Server\n475\t\n476\tThe plan originally included a coordination server with `Arc<Mutex<ServerState>>`, in-memory priority queues, heartbeats, and a Unix socket protocol. This was removed because:\n477\t\n478\t1. **SQLite is sufficient**: `BEGIN IMMEDIATE` provides serialized write access. Claim latency is ~0.5-2ms — 4-16× faster than the previous flock+streaming-rewrite approach.\n479\t2. **Simpler operations**: No flock infrastructure, no streaming rewrite, no temp files during normal operation. All mutation logic is SQL.\n480\t3. **Crash recovery is automatic**: SQLite rolls back incomplete transactions on next open. No partial state, no temp file cleanup.\n481\t4. **Real-world scale validates it**: A 2,000-bead workspace takes ~2ms per claim. A 20-worker fleet needs ~1-2 claims/sec. SQLite delivers ~500 claims/sec at that size.\n482\t5. **Server is a future optimization**: A persistent in-memory server would reduce per-claim latency from ~2ms to ~0.1ms by eliminating SQLite file I/O entirely. Premature for current scale — can be added transparently later.\n483\t\n484\t### 4.7 Comparison to br's Race Condition\n485\t\n486\t| Scenario | br (beads_rust) | bf (bead-forge) |\n487\t|----------|-----------------|-----------------|\n488\t| 20 workers claim simultaneously | 20 read same JSONL, all see same \"next\" bead, all write `in_progress` — last writer wins, 19 workers have phantom claims | 20 workers serialized by SQLite write lock — each BEGIN IMMEDIATE sees post-commit state, each gets a distinct bead |\n489\t| Worker crashes mid-claim | N/A (no lock) | SQLite rolls back the incomplete transaction automatically on next open |\n490\t| Stale claim (worker died) | Bead stays `in_progress` forever | Next claim invocation reclaims beads older than TTL |\n491\t| Cross-workspace claiming | Not supported | `--any` locks all JSONL files in sorted order |\n492\t\n493\t---\n494\t\n495\t## Phase 4B: Extended Features\n496\t\n497\t### 4B.1 JSONL Rotation (`src/rotate.rs`)\n498\t\n499\tKeep `issues.jsonl` lean for fast claiming. With one entry per bead, the file grows only when new beads are created — but over a long project lifetime the accumulation of closed beads still grows the file. Rotation evicts closed beads into numbered archive files, keeping the active scan set small.\n500\t\n501\t```\n502\t.beads/\n503\t├── issues.jsonl          # active: open, in_progress, blocked, deferred + recently closed\n504\t├── issues.jsonl.1        # archive: closed beads rotated out most recently\n505\t├── issues.jsonl.2        # archive: older closed beads\n506\t└── issues.jsonl.3        # archive: oldest\n507\t```\n508\t\n509\t**Rotation rules**:\n510\t- `bf rotate` moves closed beads older than `rotate_age_days` (default 30) from `issues.jsonl` into `issues.jsonl.1` via streaming rewrite (active file) + streaming append (archive file)\n511\t- When `issues.jsonl.1` exceeds `rotate_max_size` (default 5MB), it becomes `issues.jsonl.2`, `.2` → `.3`, etc.\n512\t- Maximum archive files: `rotate_max_archives` (default 5). Oldest archive is deleted.\n513\t\n514\t**Claim only reads the active file**. This keeps claim latency proportional to the number of active (non-closed) beads rather than total workspace history.\n515\t\n516\t**Reading archived beads**: `bf show <id>` searches the active file first, then archives. `bf list --all` includes archives. `bf log <id>` searches all files for the full history.\n517\t\n518\t**git integration**: `.beads/issues.jsonl` is committed to git. Archive files (`.jsonl.1`, `.2`, ...) are also committed — they're part of the project history. The `.beads/` directory is the bead store and should be version-controlled alongside the code.\n519\t\n520\t**Secret protection**: Before any write, bf scans bead fields for secret patterns (AWS keys, private keys, API tokens, passwords) using a built-in regex set similar to git-secrets. If a match is found:\n521\t- `bf create` / `bf update` prints a warning and refuses the write\n522\t- `bf commit-check` (git pre-commit hook) scans staged changes to `.beads/` for secrets\n523\t- Patterns are configurable in `.beads/config.yaml`:\n524\t\n525\t```yaml\n526\tsecret_protection:\n527\t  enabled: true\n528\t  # Additional patterns (matched against all string fields)\n529\t  deny_patterns:\n530\t    - \"-----BEGIN (RSA |EC |DSA )?PRIVATE KEY-----\"\n531\t    - \"AKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}\"          # AWS access key\n532\t    - \"sk-[a-zA-Z0-9]{32,}\"       # OpenAI/Anthropic API key\n533\t  # Fields to exclude from scanning (e.g., known test values)\n534\t  allowlist_fields: []\n535\t```\n536\t\n537\tConfig:\n538\t\n539\t```yaml\n540\trotate_age_days: 30\n541\trotate_max_size_mb: 5\n542\trotate_max_archives: 5\n543\t```\n544\t\n545\t### 4B.2 Batch Operations (`src/batch.rs`)\n546\t\n547\tExecute multiple operations atomically under a single SQLite `BEGIN IMMEDIATE` transaction. Replaces sequences of N separate CLI invocations with one.\n548\t\n549\t```bash\n550\t# From a file\n551\tbf batch --file operations.json\n552\t\n553\t# From stdin (one operation per line)\n554\techo 'create --title \"child 1\" --type task\n555\tcreate --title \"child 2\" --type task\n556\tdep add-blocker fg-a3f8.1 fg-a3f8\n557\tdep add-blocker fg-a3f8.2 fg-a3f8\n558\tclose fg-a3f8 --reason \"Split into children\"' | bf batch\n559\t\n560\t# Programmatic JSON input\n561\t# NEEDLE mitosis: use @0, @1 to reference created beads by position\n562\tbf batch --json '[\n563\t  {\"op\": \"create\", \"title\": \"child 1\", \"type\": \"task\"},\n564\t  {\"op\": \"create\", \"title\": \"child 2\", \"type\": \"task\"},\n565\t  {\"op\": \"dep_add_blocker\", \"parent\": \"@0\", \"child\": \"fg-a3f8\"},\n566\t  {\"op\": \"dep_add_blocker\", \"parent\": \"@1\", \"child\": \"fg-a3f8\"},\n567\t  {\"op\": \"close\", \"id\": \"fg-a3f8\", \"reason\": \"Split into children\"}\n568\t]'\n569\t```\n570\t\n571\t**Placeholder references**: `@0`, `@1`, etc. resolve to the IDs of beads created at that position. Critical for mitosis — you don't know child IDs until they're created.\n572\t\n573\t**Atomicity**: All operations run inside a single SQLite write transaction (`BEGIN IMMEDIATE`). If any operation fails (invalid ID, bad status transition), the transaction rolls back — no partial state is committed. Kill -9 during batch leaves the workspace in its original state.\n574\t\n575\t**Use cases**:\n576\t- NEEDLE mitosis: split 1 bead into N children (2N+1 operations → 1 batch)\n577\t- Bulk status changes: close all beads with a given label\n578\t- Migration: import beads from another tracker\n579\t- Workspace setup: create epics + child beads + dependencies in one shot\n580\t\n581\t**Output**: Returns JSON array of results, one per operation:\n582\t\n583\t```json\n584\t[\n585\t  {\"op\": 0, \"status\": \"ok\", \"id\": \"fg-b4d1\"},\n586\t  {\"op\": 1, \"status\": \"ok\", \"id\": \"fg-b4d2\"},\n587\t  {\"op\": 2, \"status\": \"ok\"},\n588\t  {\"op\": 3, \"status\": \"ok\"}\n589\t]\n590\t```\n591\t\n592\t### 4B.3 Annotations — Extensible Metadata (`src/model.rs`, `src/cli/annotate.rs`)\n593\t\n594\tArbitrary key-value pairs on any bead, stored in a **separate `bead_annotations` table** — never as a column on `issues`.\n595\t\n596\t**Why not a column on `issues`**: br's `issues_column_order_matches()` check in `schema.rs:630` fires on every `br` open and returns `false` the moment the actual column count differs from `EXPECTED_ISSUE_COLUMN_ORDER.len()`. This triggers `rebuild_issues_table()` which rewrites `issues` from br's canonical column list — destroying any bf-added columns. Adding an `annotations` column to `issues` would be silently wiped on every `br` invocation.\n597\t\n598\t**Schema** (br never touches this table):\n599\t\n600\t```sql\n601\tCREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS bead_annotations (\n602\t    bead_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES issues(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,\n603\t    key     TEXT NOT NULL,\n604\t    value   TEXT NOT NULL,\n605\t    PRIMARY KEY (bead_id, key)\n606\t);\n607\tCREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_bead_annotations_key_value\n608\t    ON bead_annotations (key, value);  -- for bf list --annotation key=value\n609\t```\n610\t\n611\t**Issue struct** (annotations loaded via JOIN, serialized into the JSONL field):\n612\t\n613\t```rust\n614\t#[serde(default, skip_serializing_if = \"BTreeMap::is_empty\")]\n615\tpub annotations: BTreeMap<String, String>,\n616\t```\n617\t\n618\tJSONL export serializes annotations into the `Issue` JSON object. On import, annotations are upserted into `bead_annotations`. br ignores the `annotations` JSON field when reading JSONL (`skip_unknown_fields`), so JSONL round-trips are clean.\n619\t\n620\t**CLI**:\n621\t\n622\t```bash\n623\t# Set annotations\n624\tbf annotate fg-a3f8 needle_attempt=3 needle_session=abc123\n625\tbf annotate fg-a3f8 review_status=needs_review\n626\t\n627\t# Remove annotations\n628\tbf annotate fg-a3f8 --remove needle_error\n629\t\n630\t# Query by annotation\n631\tbf list --annotation needle_attempt=3\n632\tbf list --annotation review_status=needs_review\n633\t\n634\t# Show annotations (included in bf show output)\n635\tbf show fg-a3f8 --json | jq '.annotations'\n636\t```\n637\t\n638\t**br backward compatibility**: br never reads, writes, or rebuilds `bead_annotations`. When br opens a bf-migrated database: its `issues_column_order_matches()` check passes (no extra columns on `issues`), no rebuild fires, annotation data is untouched. When br creates a bead, `bead_annotations` has no row for it — that is correct, the bead just has no annotations yet.\n639\t\n640\t**Use cases**:\n641\t- NEEDLE: `needle_attempt`, `needle_session`, `needle_error`, `needle_worker_version`\n642\t- Custom tools: `ci_build_id`, `deployment_sha`, `jira_key`\n643\t- Fleet tracking: `claimed_at`, `heartbeat_at`, `estimated_minutes_remaining`\n644\t\n645\t### 4B.4 Operation History — `bf log` (`src/log.rs`)\n646\t\n647\tShow the history of mutations on a specific bead or workspace, sourced from the SQLite events table and git history.\n648\t\n649\t```bash\n650\tbf log fg-a3f8                          # full history of this bead\n651\tbf log --since \"2026-04-25\"             # all workspace changes since date\n652\tbf log --actor worker-3                 # changes by a specific worker\n653\tbf log --limit 20                       # last 20 mutations\n654\tbf log --status-changes                 # only status transitions\n655\t```\n656\t\n657\tOutput:\n658\t\n659\t```\n660\t2026-04-25 10:00  CREATED     by: human        \"Implement auth flow\"\n661\t2026-04-25 10:05  DEP_ADD     by: human        blocked by fg-b2c1\n662\t2026-04-25 14:30  CLAIMED     by: worker-3     status: open → in_progress\n663\t2026-04-25 15:45  COMMENT     by: worker-3     \"Found edge case in token refresh\"\n664\t2026-04-25 16:00  CLOSED      by: worker-3     \"Completed\"\n665\t```\n666\t\n667\t**Implementation**: History is sourced from two places:\n668\t\n669\t1. **SQLite events table** (primary): every mutation records an event row (`event_type`, `actor`, `old_value`, `new_value`). `bf log` queries this table directly — O(1) lookup by issue ID, no file scanning.\n670\t\n671\t2. **Git history** (fallback/deep history): `issues.jsonl` is committed to git on each sync. `git log -p .beads/issues.jsonl` shows every past state of the file. `bf log --git` uses this for history older than the events table retention window.\n672\t\n673\tSince JSONL has one entry per bead (not a mutation log), JSONL diffing is not used for history. The events table is the authoritative mutation record.\n674\t\n675\t**`bf log --diff`**: Show the field-level diff from the events table:\n676\t\n677\t```bash\n678\tbf log fg-a3f8 --diff\n679\t# 2026-04-25 14:30  CLAIMED\n680\t#   - status: \"open\" → \"in_progress\"\n681\t#   + assignee: null → \"worker-3\"\n682\t#   + annotations.needle_attempt: \"1\" → \"2\"\n683\t```\n684\t\n685\t### 4B.5 Critical Path Computation (`src/critical_path.rs`)\n686\t\n687\tComputes the longest dependency chain through the workspace graph and annotates each bead with its **float** — how many hops it can slip before delaying the epic. Beads with `float == 0` are on the critical path; every day they're blocked delays completion by a day.\n688\t\n689\t**Command**:\n690\t\n691\t```bash\n692\t$ bf critical-path fg-epic-auth\n693\t\n694\tCritical path for fg-epic-auth (14 open beads, 3 on critical path):\n695\t\n696\t  float=0  [fg-a3f8] Implement auth token refresh        in_progress (worker-3)\n697\t  float=0  [fg-b2c1] Fix concurrent session state bug    open\n698\t  float=0  [fg-c4d9] Integration tests: auth flow        open\n699\t  float=2  [fg-d5e3] Update API documentation            open\n700\t  float=2  [fg-e6f4] Refactor error response format      open\n701\t  float=5  [fg-f7g5] Add rate limiting headers           open\n702\t  ...\n703\t\n704\tLongest chain: fg-a3f8 → fg-b2c1 → fg-c4d9 → [epic closes]\n705\tMinimum remaining time: 3 bead-completions on critical path\n706\t```\n707\t\n708\t**Algorithm**: Two-pass walk on the dependency DAG using SQLite recursive CTEs.\n709\t\n710\tPass 1 (forward — compute earliest start `ES`): start from beads with no open predecessors (`ES=0`). For each bead, `ES = max(ES of all open predecessors) + 1`.\n711\t\n712\tPass 2 (backward — compute latest start `LS`): start from the epic. For each bead, `LS = min(LS of all successors) - 1`. `float = LS - ES`. Zero-float beads form the critical path.\n713\t\n714\t```sql\n715\tWITH RECURSIVE\n716\t  -- Forward pass: earliest start for each open bead\n717\t  forward(id, es) AS (\n718\t    SELECT i.id, 0\n719\t    FROM issues i\n720\t    WHERE i.status IN ('open', 'in_progress')\n721\t      AND NOT EXISTS (\n722\t        SELECT 1 FROM dependencies d\n723\t        JOIN issues pred ON pred.id = d.depends_on_id\n724\t        WHERE d.issue_id = i.id AND d.type = 'blocks'\n725\t          AND pred.status IN ('open', 'in_progress')\n726\t      )\n727\t    UNION ALL\n728\t    SELECT d.issue_id, MAX(f.es) + 1\n729\t    FROM dependencies d\n730\t    JOIN forward f ON f.id = d.depends_on_id\n731\t    WHERE d.type = 'blocks'\n732\t    GROUP BY d.issue_id\n733\t  )\n734\tSELECT id, es,\n735\t       (SELECT MAX(es) FROM forward) - es AS float\n736\tFROM forward\n737\tORDER BY float ASC, es ASC;\n738\t```\n739\t\n740\t**Schema addition**: `critical_path_cache` table — recomputed whenever a dependency is added/removed or a bead changes status. Invalidated by a trigger on `dependencies` and `issues.status`.\n741\t\n742\t```sql\n743\tCREATE TABLE critical_path_cache (\n744\t    bead_id     TEXT PRIMARY KEY REFERENCES issues(id),\n745\t    epic_id     TEXT REFERENCES issues(id),\n746\t    es          INTEGER NOT NULL,   -- earliest start (hops from root)\n747\t    ls          INTEGER NOT NULL,   -- latest start\n748\t    float       INTEGER NOT NULL,   -- ls - es; 0 = critical path\n749\t    updated_at  DATETIME NOT NULL\n750\t);\n751\t```\n752\t\n753\t**Integration with claim scoring**: The claim query joins `critical_path_cache` and adds a large bonus for zero-float beads:\n754\t\n755\t```sql\n756\t1000.0 / (COALESCE(cp.float, 999) + 1) AS critical_path_bonus\n757\t```\n758\t\n759\tA zero-float bead gets `1000 / 1 = 1000` bonus. A float-5 bead gets `1000 / 6 ≈ 167`. A bead not on any epic path gets `1000 / 1000 = 1`. This ensures critical-path beads always sort above non-critical beads regardless of their listed priority.\n760\t\n761\t**`bf dep add-blocker` side effect**: Every time a dependency is created or removed, the cache for the affected epic is invalidated and recomputed inside the same `BEGIN IMMEDIATE` transaction. Recompute cost is O(V + E) — for a 500-bead workspace with 300 deps, under 1ms.\n762\t\n763\t### 4B.6 Velocity-Aware Impact Scoring (`src/velocity.rs`)\n764\t\n765\tThe claim scorer currently orders by `downstream_impact / priority`. That's correct for maximizing throughput when all workers and task types take similar time. In practice, a `claude-opus-4-7` worker closes `bug` beads in 8 minutes while the same harness on `claude-haiku-4-5` takes 35 minutes for the same type — a 4× difference. The optimal claim for each worker is the task where `impact / expected_duration` is maximized for *that specific worker configuration*, not the global impact rank.\n766\t\n767\t**Worker metadata on claim**: Workers pass their composition to `bf claim`:\n768\t\n769\t```bash\n770\tbf claim --assignee worker-01 \\\n771\t         --model claude-sonnet-4-6 \\\n772\t         --harness needle \\\n773\t         --harness-version 0.5.2 \\\n774\t         --json\n775\t```\n776\t\n777\t**Schema**:\n778\t\n779\t```sql\n780\t-- One row per worker session (created on bf claim, updated on bf close)\n781\tCREATE TABLE worker_sessions (\n782\t    session_id      TEXT PRIMARY KEY,  -- UUID generated by worker\n783\t    worker_id       TEXT NOT NULL,\n784\t    model           TEXT,              -- 'claude-sonnet-4-6', 'claude-opus-4-7', etc.\n785\t    harness         TEXT,              -- 'needle', 'custom'\n786\t    harness_version TEXT,              -- '0.5.2'\n787\t    bead_id         TEXT REFERENCES issues(id),\n788\t    claimed_at      DATETIME,\n789\t    closed_at       DATETIME,\n790\t    duration_seconds INTEGER           -- populated on bf close\n791\t);\n792\t\n793\t-- Aggregated velocity statistics per (model, harness, issue_type)\n794\tCREATE TABLE velocity_stats (\n795\t    model           TEXT NOT NULL,\n796\t    harness         TEXT NOT NULL,\n797\t    issue_type      TEXT NOT NULL,\n798\t    sample_count    INTEGER DEFAULT 0,\n799\t    p50_seconds     INTEGER,\n800\t    p90_seconds     INTEGER,\n801\t    avg_seconds     REAL,\n802\t    last_updated    DATETIME,\n803\t    PRIMARY KEY (model, harness, issue_type)\n804\t);\n805\t```\n806\t\n807\t**Update on close**: When `bf close <id>` is called, if the closing worker passed `--session-id <uuid>`, the session row is updated with `closed_at` and `duration_seconds`. A trigger recomputes `velocity_stats` for that `(model, harness, issue_type)` tuple using a window over the last 50 sessions.\n808\t\n809\t**Claim scoring integration**: The claim query joins `velocity_stats` for the requesting worker and computes `score = impact / expected_seconds`:\n810\t\n811\t```sql\n812\tSELECT\n813\t    i.id,\n814\t    i.priority,\n815\t    i.issue_type,\n816\t    COALESCE(cp.float, 999)            AS critical_float,\n817\t    COALESCE(vs.p50_seconds, 1800)     AS expected_seconds,\n818\t    -- Combined score: more impact per unit time = higher priority\n819\t    (\n820\t      downstream_impact * 3.0\n821\t      + (4 - i.priority) * 2.0\n822\t      + 1000.0 / (COALESCE(cp.float, 999) + 1)\n823\t    ) / COALESCE(vs.p50_seconds, 1800) AS score\n824\tFROM issues i\n825\tLEFT JOIN critical_path_cache cp  ON cp.bead_id = i.id\n826\tLEFT JOIN velocity_stats vs       ON vs.issue_type = i.issue_type\n827\t                                 AND vs.model    = ?model\n828\t                                 AND vs.harness  = ?harness\n829\tWHERE i.status = 'open'\n830\t  AND i.id NOT IN (SELECT issue_id FROM blocked_issues_cache)\n831\tORDER BY score DESC\n832\tLIMIT 1\n833\t```\n834\t\n835\tWorkers with no velocity history use the 1800s (30-minute) default — conservative but correct. After ~10 completions per type, the estimate is meaningful.\n836\t\n837\t**`bf velocity` command**: Show the velocity table for all known worker configurations:\n838\t\n839\t```\n840\t$ bf velocity\n841\t\n842\tModel                  Harness   Type     Samples  p50    p90\n843\tclaude-opus-4-7        needle    task     142      8m     22m\n844\tclaude-opus-4-7        needle    bug      38       14m    51m\n845\tclaude-sonnet-4-6      needle    task     87       18m    45m\n846\tclaude-sonnet-4-6      needle    bug      24       31m    1h12m\n847\tclaude-haiku-4-5       needle    task     23       35m    2h10m\n848\t```\n849\t\n850\tThis table surfaces which model+harness combinations are most efficient for which task types — direct input for fleet composition decisions (which models to provision for which workspace).\n851\t\n852\t**Fallback**: If `--model` is not passed to `bf claim`, the scorer uses the average across all models for that issue type. Backward compatible — undecorated `bf claim` calls work exactly as before, just without per-model scoring.\n853\t\n854\t---\n855\t\n856\t## Phase 4C: br→bf Workspace Migration (`src/migrate.rs`)\n857\t\n858\tExisting repos using `br` migrate to `bf` without data loss. Three migration paths cover all scenarios.\n859\t\n860\t### Migration Path A — Drop-in Replace (zero downtime, zero data conversion)\n861\t\n862\tThe `br`→`bf` symlink is the migration for most repos:\n863\t\n864\t```bash\n865\tln -sf ~/.local/bin/bf ~/.local/bin/br\n866\t```\n867\t\n868\t`apply_migrations()` in `Storage::open` handles schema differences automatically on first open:\n869\t\n870\t```rust\n871\tfn apply_migrations(conn: &Connection) -> Result<()> {\n872\t    // All bf-only tables use CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS — no-op on existing DBs.\n873\t    // Annotations are stored in bead_annotations, NOT as a column on issues.\n874\t    // Reason: br's issues_column_order_matches() in schema.rs:630 fires on every\n875\t    // br open and triggers rebuild_issues_table() if the column count differs —\n876\t    // which silently destroys any extra column. bead_annotations is a separate\n877\t    // table br never reads, writes, or rebuilds.\n878\t    conn.execute_batch(\"\n879\t        CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS bead_annotations (\n880\t            bead_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES issues(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,\n881\t            key     TEXT NOT NULL,\n882\t            value   TEXT NOT NULL,\n883\t            PRIMARY KEY (bead_id, key)\n884\t        );\n885\t        CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_bead_annotations_key_value\n886\t            ON bead_annotations (key, value);\n887\t        CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS worker_sessions ( ... );\n888\t        CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS velocity_stats ( ... );\n889\t        CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS critical_path_cache ( ... );\n890\t    \")?;\n891\t    Ok(())\n892\t}\n893\t```\n894\t\n895\tAll bf-only tables are created with `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS` — the call is a no-op when the database was already created by `bf`. No column migrations on `issues` are needed.\n896\t\n897\t**br backward compatibility after migration**: `br` opens a bf-migrated database and finds the `issues` table column count matches exactly — `issues_column_order_matches()` passes, `rebuild_issues_table()` never fires. br ignores `bead_annotations`, `worker_sessions`, `velocity_stats`, and `critical_path_cache` entirely. All `br` commands (list, show, update, doctor) continue working.\n898\t\n899\t### Migration Path B — `bf migrate` (explicit, with backup and verification)\n900\t\n901\tFor workspaces where you want an audit point and rollback safety:\n902\t\n903\t```bash\n904\tbf migrate /path/to/workspace [--dry-run]\n905\t```\n906\t\n907\tSteps:\n908\t1. **Pause fleet** (optional): write a `migration_lock` row to the workspace DB so `bf claim` refuses new claims during migration\n909\t2. **Backup**: `cp beads.db beads.db.br-backup-<timestamp>`\n910\t3. **Apply migrations**: call `apply_migrations()` explicitly\n911\t4. **Prime caches**: populate `critical_path_cache` for all epics in one pass\n912\t5. **Seed config**: add `bf`-specific keys with defaults to `config.yaml` if absent:\n913\t   ```yaml\n914\t   # bead-forge additions (br ignores these)\n915\t   claim_ttl_minutes: 30\n916\t   rotate_age_days: 30\n917\t   rotate_max_size_mb: 5\n918\t   ```\n919\t6. **Verify forward compat**: run `br doctor --db beads.db` — must exit 0\n920\t7. **Verify backward compat**: run `bf doctor --check` — must exit 0\n921\t8. **Release fleet**: remove `migration_lock`\n922\t\n923\tOutput:\n924\t```\n925\tMigrating /home/coding/FORGE/.beads\n926\t  ✓ Backed up beads.db → beads.db.br-backup-2026-04-30T09:15:00Z\n927\t  ✓ Applied schema migrations (created bead_annotations, worker_sessions, velocity_stats, critical_path_cache tables)\n928\t  ✓ Primed critical_path_cache for 3 epics (247 beads)\n929\t  ✓ Updated config.yaml with bf defaults\n930\t  ✓ br doctor: OK\n931\t  ✓ bf doctor: OK\n932\tMigration complete. br symlink: ln -sf ~/.local/bin/bf ~/.local/bin/br\n933\t```\n934\t\n935\t### Migration Path C — `bf migrate --from-jsonl` (events table lost)\n936\t\n937\tWhen `beads.db` is missing or corrupted beyond repair and only `issues.jsonl` is available (the `br doctor --repair` scenario). The standard reimport loses the `events` table — all claim/close history vanishes.\n938\t\n939\t`--from-jsonl` recovers history from git:\n940\t\n941\t```bash\n942\tbf migrate /path/to/workspace --from-jsonl [--seed-velocity]\n943\t```\n944\t\n945\t1. **Reimport**: stream `issues.jsonl` into fresh SQLite (standard import)\n946\t2. **Reconstruct events from git log**:\n947\t   ```bash\n948\t   git log --follow -p .beads/issues.jsonl\n949\t   ```\n950\t   For each commit that touched `issues.jsonl`:\n951\t   - Diff the JSONL before/after\n952\t   - New bead appearing → synthetic `created` event with commit timestamp + author\n953\t   - Bead status changing `open→in_progress` → synthetic `claimed` event\n954\t   - Bead status changing `in_progress→closed` → synthetic `closed` event + compute `duration_seconds`\n955\t   - Synthetic events get `metadata: {\"source\": \"git-reconstructed\", \"commit\": \"<sha>\"}`\n956\t3. **Seed velocity stats** (`--seed-velocity`): from reconstructed `closed` events with known actor, compute `(model=unknown, harness=unknown, issue_type)` averages. Actor name patterns (`worker-claude-sonnet-4-6-01`) can infer model if the naming convention was consistent.\n957\t\n958\tRecovery completeness depends on how often `bf sync` was run (each sync = one JSONL commit in git). With daily syncs: ~95% of events reconstructed. With hourly syncs: ~99%.\n959\t\n960\t### Migration Checklist for All Repos\n961\t\n962\t```bash\n963\t# Per-machine: install bf, symlink br\n964\tcurl -L https://github.com/jedarden/bead-forge/releases/latest/download/bf-linux-x86_64 \\\n965\t  -o ~/.local/bin/bf && chmod +x ~/.local/bin/bf\n966\tln -sf ~/.local/bin/bf ~/.local/bin/br\n967\t\n968\t# Per-workspace: explicit migration with backup\n969\tfor workspace in \\\n970\t  /home/coding/FORGE \\\n971\t  /home/coding/NEEDLE \\\n972\t  /home/coding/AgentScribe \\\n973\t  /home/coding/ARMOR \\\n974\t  /home/coding/SIGIL \\\n975\t  /home/coding/CLASP \\\n976\t  /home/coding/bead-forge; do\n977\t  bf migrate \"$workspace\"\n978\tdone\n979\t\n980\t# Update NEEDLE config to pass worker metadata\n981\t# In .config/needle/adapters/claude-sonnet.yaml, add to invoke_template:\n982\t#   bf claim --model claude-sonnet-4-6 --harness needle --harness-version 0.5.2 ...\n983\t```\n984\t\n985\t### Schema Compatibility Matrix\n986\t\n987\t| Scenario | br behavior | Result |\n988\t|----------|-------------|--------|\n989\t| br opens bf-created DB | `issues` column count matches br's expected count exactly (no extra columns); br ignores `bead_annotations`, `worker_sessions`, `velocity_stats`, `critical_path_cache` | ✓ Full compat |\n990\t| bf opens br-created DB | `apply_migrations()` creates `bead_annotations` + bf-only tables via `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS` | ✓ Transparent |\n991\t| bf opens very old br DB (missing `issues` columns) | br's own `rebuild_issues_table()` handles this on next `br` open; bf schema also applies its own `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS` for bf-only tables | ✓ Handled |\n992\t| br and bf open same DB concurrently | `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS` is idempotent; WAL serializes writes | ✓ Safe |\n993\t| bf opens bf-created DB | `apply_migrations()` is no-op — all tables already exist | ✓ Idempotent |\n994\t\n995\t---\n996\t\n997\t## Phase 5: NEEDLE Integration\n998\t\n999\tNEEDLE's `bead_store/mod.rs` chains several `br` CLI calls that have race windows between them. Each race is eliminated by the corresponding `bf` command.\n1000\t\n1001\t### 5.1 Race 1 — `claim()`: two-process verify (lines 518-558)\n1002\t\n1003\t**br (racy)**:\n1004\t```rust\n1005\t// Call 1: br update <id> --status in_progress --assignee <actor>\n1006\t// ← race window: another worker sees this bead as open and claims it ←\n1007\t// Call 2: br show <id>  (to verify .assignee == actor)\n1008\t```\n1009\tTwo processes, two SQLite opens. If two workers race, both issue `br update` and `br` does not enforce exclusivity — it just overwrites. The `br show` verify is too late: the wrong worker may have overwritten the `assignee` field between the two calls.\n1010\t\n1011\t**bf (atomic)**:\n1012\t```bash\n1013\tbead=$(bf claim --workspace \"$WORKSPACE\" --assignee \"$WORKER\" --json)\n1014\t```\n1015\t`bf claim` runs the full read-score-update sequence inside a single `BEGIN IMMEDIATE` transaction. No second call, no verify step needed.\n1016\t\n1017\t### 5.2 Race 2 — `ready()` → `claim()`: TOCTOU (lines 487-507)\n1018\t\n1019\t**br (racy)**:\n1020\t```rust\n1021\t// Call 1: br ready --json  → returns candidate list (a snapshot)\n1022\t// ← race window: another worker claims the top candidate ←\n1023\t// Call 2: claim(selected_id)  → may be claiming an already-claimed bead\n1024\t```\n1025\tThe winning bead is selected from a stale snapshot. By the time `claim()` issues `br update`, the bead may have been claimed by another worker.\n1026\t\n1027\t**bf (atomic)**:\n1028\t```bash\n1029\tbead=$(bf claim --workspace \"$WORKSPACE\" --assignee \"$WORKER\" --json)\n1030\t```\n1031\tScoring and claiming happen inside the same `BEGIN IMMEDIATE` transaction. The candidate list is read and the winner is updated atomically — no snapshot staleness.\n1032\t\n1033\t### 5.3 Race 3 — Mitosis: `create_bead` + `add_dependency` (lines 607-639)\n1034\t\n1035\t**br (non-atomic)**:\n1036\t```rust\n1037\t// Call 1: br create \"child title\"  → returns new bead ID\n1038\t// ← process crash here → orphaned bead with no dependency link ←\n1039\t// Call 2: br dep add <parent_id> <child_id>\n1040\t```\n1041\tIf NEEDLE dies between `br create` and `br dep add`, the child bead exists but is not linked to the parent. The parent is never closed; the orphaned child is never picked up.\n1042\t\n1043\t**bf (atomic)**:\n1044\t```bash\n1045\tbf batch --json '[\n1046\t  {\"op\": \"create\", \"title\": \"child 1\", \"type\": \"task\"},\n1047\t  {\"op\": \"create\", \"title\": \"child 2\", \"type\": \"task\"},\n1048\t  {\"op\": \"dep_add_blocker\", \"parent\": \"fg-a3f8.1\", \"child\": \"fg-a3f8\"},\n1049\t  {\"op\": \"dep_add_blocker\", \"parent\": \"fg-a3f8.2\", \"child\": \"fg-a3f8\"},\n1050\t  {\"op\": \"close\", \"id\": \"fg-a3f8\", \"reason\": \"Split into children\"}\n1051\t]'\n1052\t```\n1053\tAll five operations run inside one `BEGIN IMMEDIATE` transaction. If NEEDLE dies mid-batch, the transaction rolls back automatically — no partial state.\n1054\t\n1055\t### 5.4 Inefficiency — `labels()` round-trip (lines 584-588)\n1056\t\n1057\t**br (wasteful)**:\n1058\t```rust\n1059\tasync fn labels(&self, id: &BeadId) -> Result<Vec<String>> {\n1060\t    let bead = self.show(id).await?;  // full br show just to get .labels\n1061\t    Ok(bead.labels)\n1062\t}\n1063\t```\n1064\tOne full `br show` (with JSON deserialization of all 35 fields) to extract one field.\n1065\t\n1066\t**bf (direct)**:\n1067\t```bash\n1068\tbf labels <id> --json   # SELECT label FROM labels WHERE issue_id = ?\n1069\t```\n1070\tDirect `SELECT` on the `labels` table. Single column, single round-trip.\n1071\t\n1072\t### 5.5 Inefficiency — Multiple `br label add` calls\n1073\t\n1074\t**br (unbatched)**:\n1075\t```rust\n1076\tfor label in &labels {\n1077\t    br_label_add(id, label).await?;   // separate process per label\n1078\t}\n1079\t```\n1080\tN labels = N process invocations = N SQLite open/close cycles.\n1081\t\n1082\t**bf (batched)**:\n1083\t```bash\n1084\tbf label <id> add label1 label2 label3   # one INSERT per label, one transaction\n1085\t```\n1086\t\n1087\t### 5.6 Pluck Strand Update\n1088\t\n1089\tOne-line change in NEEDLE's `pluck.sh` or `bead_store/mod.rs`:\n1090\t\n1091\t```bash\n1092\t# Old (racy — two br calls with a race window between them):\n1093\t# br ready --json | pick_top | xargs br update --status in_progress --assignee \"$WORKER\"\n1094\t\n1095\t# New (atomic — single bf call, BEGIN IMMEDIATE claim):\n1096\tbead=$(bf claim --workspace \"$WORKSPACE\" --assignee \"$WORKER\" --json)\n1097\t```\n1098\t\n1099\t### 5.7 Close/Block Remains Same\n1100\t\n1101\t```bash\n1102\tbf close \"$bead_id\" --reason \"Completed: ...\"\n1103\tbf update \"$bead_id\" --status blocked\n1104\t```\n1105\t\n1106\tThese are single-operation writes with no race condition — no change needed.\n1107\t\n1108\t### 5.8 Symlink for Backward Compatibility\n1109\t\n1110\t```bash\n1111\tln -sf ~/.local/bin/bf ~/.local/bin/br\n1112\t```\n1113\t\n1114\tAll existing scripts using `br` commands work without modification. NEEDLE's non-atomic chains are replaced incrementally — `bf claim` and `bf batch` are the high-priority fixes; the rest can migrate when convenient.\n1115\t\n1116\t---\n1117\t\n1118\t## Phase 6: Build & Deploy\n1119\t\n1120\t### 6.1 Project Structure\n1121\t\n1122\t```\n1123\tbead-forge/\n1124\t├── Cargo.toml\n1125\t├── src/\n1126\t│   ├── main.rs              # CLI entry point\n1127\t│   ├── lib.rs               # Library root\n1128\t│   ├── model.rs             # Issue, Status, Priority, Annotations\n1129\t│   ├── jsonl.rs             # JSONL import (sync --import) + export (sync --flush)\n1130\t│   ├── config.rs            # Config + Metadata parsing\n1131\t│   ├── id.rs                # ID generation\n1132\t│   ├── claim.rs             # Atomic BEGIN IMMEDIATE claiming + impact scoring\n1133\t│   ├── rotate.rs            # JSONL log rotation\n1134\t│   ├── batch.rs             # Batch operations under single BEGIN IMMEDIATE transaction\n1135\t│   ├── log.rs               # Operation history from JSONL event log\n1136\t│   ├── secrets.rs           # Secret pattern scanning\n1137\t│   ├── cli/\n1138\t│   │   ├── mod.rs           # clap app definition\n1139\t│   │   ├── create.rs\n1140\t│   │   ├── list.rs\n1141\t│   │   ├── show.rs\n1142\t│   │   ├── update.rs\n1143\t│   │   ├── close.rs\n1144\t│   │   ├── reopen.rs\n1145\t│   │   ├── delete.rs\n1146\t│   │   ├── ready.rs\n1147\t│   │   ├── claim.rs         # `bf claim` subcommand\n1148\t│   │   ├── annotate.rs      # `bf annotate` subcommand\n1149\t│   │   ├── batch.rs         # `bf batch` subcommand\n1150\t│   │   ├── log.rs           # `bf log` subcommand\n1151\t│   │   ├── rotate.rs        # `bf rotate` subcommand\n1152\t│   │   ├── dep.rs\n1153\t│   │   ├── label.rs\n1154\t│   │   ├── comment.rs\n1155\t│   │   ├── sync.rs\n1156\t│   │   ├── doctor.rs\n1157\t│   │   ├── init.rs\n1158\t│   │   ├── search.rs\n1159\t│   │   └── stats.rs\n1160\t│   ├── storage/\n1161\t│   │   ├── mod.rs\n1162\t│   │   ├── schema.rs        # SQLite DDL (identical to br)\n1163\t│   │   └── sqlite.rs        # rusqlite storage backend\n1164\t│   └── format/\n1165\t│       ├── mod.rs\n1166\t│       ├── text.rs\n1167\t│       ├── json.rs\n1168\t│       └── toon.rs\n1169\t├── tests/\n1170\t│   ├── common/\n1171\t│   │   └── mod.rs           # Shared test harness — TempWorkspace, fixture loading\n1172\t│   ├── jsonl_compat.rs      # Round-trip tests with br JSONL files (read-only fixtures)\n1173\t│   ├── schema_compat.rs     # Schema match + forward-compat: bf writes, br reads\n1174\t│   ├── claim_race.rs        # Concurrent BEGIN IMMEDIATE claim tests (isolated tempdir)\n1175\t│   ├── batch_atomic.rs      # Batch rollback on failure\n1176\t│   ├── br_isolation.rs      # Verify bf never touches live br workspaces\n1177\t│   └── secret_scanning.rs   # Secret pattern detection\n1178\t└── build.rs\n1179\t```\n1180\t\n1181\t### 6.2 Dependencies\n1182\t\n1183\t```toml\n1184\t[dependencies]\n1185\tclap = { version = \"4\", features = [\"derive\"] }\n1186\tserde = { version = \"1\", features = [\"derive\"] }\n1187\tserde_json = \"1\"\n1188\tserde_yaml = \"0.9\"\n1189\tchrono = { version = \"0.4\", features = [\"serde\"] }\n1190\trusqlite = { version = \"0.31\", features = [\"bundled\"] }\n1191\tsha2 = \"0.10\"\n1192\trand = \"0.8\"\n1193\tregex = \"1\"                 # Secret pattern scanning\n1194\ttracing = \"0.1\"\n1195\ttracing-subscriber = \"0.3\"\n1196\tanyhow = \"1\"\n1197\tthiserror = \"1\"\n1198\t\n1199\t[dev-dependencies]\n1200\ttempfile = \"3\"              # Ephemeral test workspaces — never touch live br workspaces\n1201\t```\n1202\t\n1203\t### 6.3 CI via Argo Workflows\n1204\t\n1205\tAdd a `bead-forge-build` WorkflowTemplate to `jedarden/declarative-config`. Builds the Rust binary in a container, pushes to `ronaldraygun/bead-forge`.\n1206\t\n1207\t### 6.4 Deployment\n1208\t\n1209\tDeploy as a standalone binary to the Hetzner server at `~/.local/bin/bf`. Symlink `br` -> `bf` for backward compatibility. No daemon, no systemd unit — each `bf` invocation is self-contained.\n1210\t\n1211\t---\n1212\t\n1213\t## Test Strategy & Isolation\n1214\t\n1215\tbf must be testable without touching, modifying, or interfering with any live `br` workspace. Three isolation concerns drive the test strategy:\n1216\t\n1217\t### Rule 1: Never Share a Live Database\n1218\t\n1219\tbf tests **never** auto-discover or open a `.beads/beads.db` that `br` is actively using. SQLite WAL mode serializes writers, so concurrent opens are safe from a corruption standpoint — but test mutations (creates, claims, closes) would contaminate real workspaces with spurious beads.\n1220\t\n1221\tAll tests target exactly one of:\n1222\t- **`:memory:`** — rusqlite in-memory database (unit tests, zero disk I/O)\n1223\t- **`tempfile::tempdir()`** — ephemeral directory deleted on drop (integration tests)\n1224\t- **Read-only fixture copy** — `issues.jsonl` copied out of a real workspace into a tempdir, never written back (compat tests)\n1225\t\n1226\t### Rule 2: Never Point `br` at a bf Test Database\n1227\t\n1228\tThe schema compat test must verify `br` can read a `bf`-created database — but it cannot use br's own workspace for this. The test creates a fresh tempdir, runs `bf init` + `bf create` in it, then invokes `br --db /path/to/temp/beads.db` against that file. The real `br` workspace is untouched.\n1229\t\n1230\t### Rule 3: JSONL Fixture = Read-Only Copy\n1231\t\n1232\tThe JSONL round-trip test uses a snapshot of a real workspace's `issues.jsonl` as a fixture, not a live file. The fixture is checked into `tests/fixtures/` (or copied at test time with `--allow-stale`). `bf sync --import` loads it into a tempdir database; `bf list --format json` is compared against the expected output derived from `br list --format json` run against the same snapshot.\n1233\t\n1234\t```\n1235\ttests/fixtures/\n1236\t├── forge-snapshot.jsonl    # snapshot of FORGE workspace (committed, static)\n1237\t└── needle-snapshot.jsonl   # snapshot of NEEDLE workspace\n1238\t```\n1239\t\n1240\tThese fixtures are updated manually when the schema changes: `cp /home/coding/FORGE/.beads/issues.jsonl tests/fixtures/forge-snapshot.jsonl`.\n1241\t\n1242\t### Test Harness (`tests/common/mod.rs`)\n1243\t\n1244\t```rust\n1245\tpub struct TempWorkspace {\n1246\t    dir: tempfile::TempDir,   // deleted on drop — no cleanup needed\n1247\t    pub db_path: PathBuf,\n1248\t    pub storage: Storage,\n1249\t}\n1250\t\n1251\timpl TempWorkspace {\n1252\t    pub fn new() -> Result<Self> {\n1253\t        let dir = tempfile::tempdir()?;\n1254\t        let db_path = dir.path().join(\".beads/beads.db\");\n1255\t        fs::create_dir(dir.path().join(\".beads\"))?;\n1256\t        let storage = Storage::open(&db_path)?;\n1257\t        Ok(Self { dir, db_path, storage })\n1258\t    }\n1259\t\n1260\t    pub fn from_fixture(jsonl: &Path) -> Result<Self> {\n1261\t        let ws = Self::new()?;\n1262\t        import_jsonl(jsonl, &ws.storage)?;\n1263\t        Ok(ws)\n1264\t    }\n1265\t\n1266\t    // Run br against this workspace's db (for compat tests)\n1267\t    pub fn br(&self, args: &[&str]) -> std::process::Output {\n1268\t        Command::new(\"br\")\n1269\t            .arg(\"--db\").arg(&self.db_path)\n1270\t            .args(args)\n1271\t            .output()\n1272\t            .expect(\"br not in PATH\")\n1273\t    }\n1274\t}\n1275\t```\n1276\t\n1277\tUnit tests in `#[cfg(test)]` modules use `Storage::open(\":memory:\")` directly — no `TempWorkspace` needed.\n1278\t\n1279\t### Schema Forward-Compatibility\n1280\t\n1281\tbf adds tables that br doesn't know about. No columns are added to the shared `issues` table.\n1282\t\n1283\t| Addition | br behavior |\n1284\t|----------|-------------|\n1285\t| `bead_annotations` table | br ignores unknown tables entirely; `issues` column count is unchanged so `issues_column_order_matches()` passes and `rebuild_issues_table()` never fires |\n1286\t| `worker_sessions` table | Same — ignored |\n1287\t| `velocity_stats` table | Same — ignored |\n1288\t| `critical_path_cache` table | Same — ignored |\n1289\t\n1290\t**Why no column on `issues`**: br's `issues_column_order_matches()` in `schema.rs:630` checks `actual_columns.len() != EXPECTED_ISSUE_COLUMN_ORDER.len()`. Any extra column causes this to return `false`, which triggers `rebuild_issues_table()` — a full DROP + recreate using only br's canonical column list. An `annotations` column on `issues` would be silently destroyed on every `br` open. Storing annotations in `bead_annotations` sidesteps this entirely.\n1291\t\n1292\tMigration on `Storage::open` (fully idempotent — all `CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS`):\n1293\t\n1294\t```rust\n1295\tfn apply_migrations(conn: &Connection) -> Result<()> {\n1296\t    conn.execute_batch(\"\n1297\t        CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS bead_annotations (\n1298\t            bead_id TEXT NOT NULL REFERENCES issues(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,\n1299\t            key     TEXT NOT NULL,\n1300\t            value   TEXT NOT NULL,\n1301\t            PRIMARY KEY (bead_id, key)\n1302\t        );\n1303\t        CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_bead_annotations_key_value\n1304\t            ON bead_annotations (key, value);\n1305\t        -- other bf-only tables follow\n1306\t    \")?;\n1307\t    Ok(())\n1308\t}\n1309\t```\n1310\t\n1311\tNo `ALTER TABLE` on `issues`, ever. The migration is a pure table-creation pass.\n1312\t\n1313\t---\n1314\t\n1315\t## Compatibility Verification\n1316\t\n1317\tAll tests run against **isolated temp workspaces** — no live br workspace is touched.\n1318\t\n1319\t### JSONL Round-Trip Test\n1320\t\n1321\t```bash\n1322\t# 1. Copy real workspace JSONL as a read-only fixture (never modify the original)\n1323\tFIXTURE=$(mktemp -d)\n1324\tcp /home/coding/FORGE/.beads/issues.jsonl \"$FIXTURE/issues.jsonl\"\n1325\t\n1326\t# 2. bf imports the fixture into a fresh tempdir\n1327\tBFDIR=$(mktemp -d)\n1328\tbf init --prefix fg --db \"$BFDIR/beads.db\"\n1329\tbf sync --import-only --db \"$BFDIR/beads.db\" --jsonl \"$FIXTURE/issues.jsonl\"\n1330\t\n1331\t# 3. bf exports its view of the data\n1332\tbf list --format json --db \"$BFDIR/beads.db\" | jq -S '.' > /tmp/bf-output.jsonl\n1333\t\n1334\t# 4. br reads the original JSONL directly (read-only, not our tempdir)\n1335\tbr list --format json --db \"$FIXTURE/issues.jsonl\" --no-db | jq -S '.' > /tmp/br-output.jsonl\n1336\t\n1337\t# 5. Compare (order-independent by ID)\n1338\tdiff /tmp/bf-output.jsonl /tmp/br-output.jsonl\n1339\t# Must be empty — bf and br produce identical output from the same JSONL\n1340\t\n1341\t# Cleanup\n1342\trm -rf \"$FIXTURE\" \"$BFDIR\"\n1343\t```\n1344\t\n1345\t### Schema Forward-Compatibility Test\n1346\t\n1347\t```bash\n1348\t# 1. bf creates a workspace in a fresh tempdir (bf schema, including bf-only tables)\n1349\tBFDIR=$(mktemp -d)\n1350\tbf init --prefix fg --db \"$BFDIR/beads.db\"\n1351\tbf create --title \"compat test\" --type task --priority 1 --db \"$BFDIR/beads.db\"\n1352\tbf create --title \"another bead\" --type bug --priority 0 --db \"$BFDIR/beads.db\"\n1353\tbf annotate fg-xxx needle_attempt=1 --db \"$BFDIR/beads.db\"\n1354\t# Annotation is stored in bead_annotations table, NOT in issues column\n1355\t\n1356\t# 2. br opens the same file (not its own workspace — pointed explicitly at the temp db)\n1357\tbr list --db \"$BFDIR/beads.db\"         # must list 2 beads without error\n1358\tbr doctor --db \"$BFDIR/beads.db\"       # must pass integrity check\n1359\t# Key assertion: br's issues_column_order_matches() sees exact column count → passes\n1360\t# rebuild_issues_table() is NOT triggered → bead_annotations table untouched\n1361\tbr update fg-xxx --status closed --db \"$BFDIR/beads.db\"  #
